coredata.dc:title: Development of a fluorescence immunoassay for highly sensitive detection of amantadine using the nanoassembly of carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets as the signal probe
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Baolei
Li, Hongfang
Sun, Jiefang
Mari, Ghulam Mujtaba
Yu, Xuezhi
Ke, Yuebin
Li, Jinfeng
Wang, Zhanhui
Yu, Wenbo
Wen, Kai
Shen, Jianzhong
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence immunoassays are rapid, convenient and cost-effective for the sensitive quantitation of chemical contaminants in foodstuff. In this study, a competitive ﬂuorescence ELISA was developed for the sensitive detection of amantadine (AMD) based on the alkaline phosphatase (ALP)-triggered fluorescence “turn-on” signals. As a fluorescence substrate, carbon dots (CDs) were adsorbed onto the surface of the MnO2 nanosheets (NSs) and formed a nanoassembly of p-CDs@MnO2 NSs which results in the fluorescence quench of CDs. The ALP labelled on antibody could catalyze the hydrolysis of the 2-phosphoascorbic acid into ascorbic acid. The latter could then reduce and decompose the MnO2 NSs, which was accompanied by the release of CDs from the surface of MnO2 NSs and led to the fluorescence recovery of CDs. The change of the fluorescence intensity is related to the concentration of AMD in solution and thus could be applied to detect AMD in an ALP-based ELISA system. The ﬂuorescent ELISA showed a linear detection for AMD in the range of 0.048 ng mL−1 to 1.1 ng mL−1 with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.035 ng mL−1. The novel ﬂuorescent ELISA shows potential for the highly sensitive detection of AMD and other analytes in food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Manganese dioxide nanosheets
Fluorescent immunoassay
Amantadine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the nanoassembly of CDs and MnO2 NSs
Synthesis and characterization of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly
Fluorescence sensing of ALP based on the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption of MnO2 nanosheets (photograph in right is MnO2 nanosheets under visible light.
(b) TEM image of MnO2 nanosheets.
(c) AFM images of MnO2 nanosheets; Red lines are the height-proﬁles analysis along the write corresponding line.
(d) Excitation–emission matrix map for p-CDs.
(e) TEM image of p-CDs (the photograph in right top is the HRTEM of p-CDs, lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.30 nm).
(f) AFM images of p-CDs; Red lines are the height-proﬁles analysis along the write corresponding line.
(F) DLS analysis of p-CDs, MnO2 nanosheets and p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly: (a) average Zeta potential; (b) average size distribution.

(a) Fluorescence spectra (red line for excitation and blue line for emission) of p-CDs. (Photograph in right top, from left to right: MnO2 nanosheets, p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly, p-CDs under visible light. Photograph in right bottom, from left to right: MnO2 nanosheets, p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly, p-CDs under 365 nm UV light). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(b) TEM image of the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly.
(c) HRTEM image of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly.
(d) AFM images of the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly; Red lines are the height-proﬁles analysis along the write corresponding line.
(e) FT-IR spectra of p-CDs (a, blue line), MnO2 nanosheet (b, purple line) and the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly (c, red line).
(f) Raman spectra of MnO2 nanosheets (black line) and the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly (red line).
(g) XPS spectra of MnO2 nanosheet (black line) and the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly (red line) in the wide scan survey.

(a) Fluorescent lifetime decay curves of p-CDs (5.61 ns, blue) and p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly (3.17 ns, black), (λex = 360 nm and λem = 450 nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(b) The fluorescence intensities of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly in the presence of diﬀ ;erent concentrations of AA and AAP (0 μM – 160 μM).

(c) The fluorescence intensity changing of the sensing system to diﬀ ;erent concentrations of ALP (0 mU mL−1–90 mU mL−1). The inset showed the plot of the ﬂuorescence intensity versus ALP concentration (0 mU mL−1–20 mU mL−1, linear equation is F = 53.88 + 62.89 C [ALP], R2 = 0.986), (λex = 360 nm and λem = 450 nm).
(a) Calibration curve of ﬂuorescent ELISA and ELISA.

(b) Speciﬁcity of ﬂuorescent ELISA against AMD, ribavirin, moroxydine, acyclovir, acetaminophen, chloramphenicol, enrofloxacin, and sulfamethazine. All the spiked concentration was 1.0 ng mL−1; Blank samples were Tris buffer containing 7% (v/v) methanol. Vertical bars indicate the standard deviation (n = 6).
(c) Recovery and precision of the ﬂuorescent ELISA from AMD spiked samples, intra-assay (n = 3) and inter-assay (n = 6).
Principle of the fluorescent immunoassay.
An overview on recently reported immunoassays for the detection of AMD.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Cut-off value was defined as the minimum concentration of AMD to produce invisible test lines in LFIA.

head.ce:title: Development of a fluorescence immunoassay for highly sensitive detection of amantadine using the nanoassembly of carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets as the signal probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, China Agricultural University, Beijing Key Laboratory of Detection Technology for Animal-Derived Food Safety, Beijing Laboratory for Food Quality and Safety, Beijing, 100193, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health
Beijing Key Laboratory of Detection Technology for Animal-Derived Food Safety
Beijing Laboratory for Food Quality and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Diagnostic and Traceability for Food Poisoning, Beijing Research Center for Preventive Medicine, Beijing, 100013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Diagnostic and Traceability for Food Poisoning
Beijing Research Center for Preventive Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Genetic Toxicology, Shenzhen Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Shenzhen, 518020, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence immunoassays are rapid, convenient and cost-effective for the sensitive quantitation of chemical contaminants in foodstuff. In this study, a competitive ﬂuorescence ELISA was developed for the sensitive detection of amantadine (AMD) based on the alkaline phosphatase (ALP)-triggered fluorescence “turn-on” signals. As a fluorescence substrate, carbon dots (CDs) were adsorbed onto the surface of the MnO2 nanosheets (NSs) and formed a nanoassembly of p-CDs@MnO2 NSs which results in the fluorescence quench of CDs. The ALP labelled on antibody could catalyze the hydrolysis of the 2-phosphoascorbic acid into ascorbic acid. The latter could then reduce and decompose the MnO2 NSs, which was accompanied by the release of CDs from the surface of MnO2 NSs and led to the fluorescence recovery of CDs. The change of the fluorescence intensity is related to the concentration of AMD in solution and thus could be applied to detect AMD in an ALP-based ELISA system. The ﬂuorescent ELISA showed a linear detection for AMD in the range of 0.048 ng mL−1 to 1.1 ng mL−1 with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.035 ng mL−1. The novel ﬂuorescent ELISA shows potential for the highly sensitive detection of AMD and other analytes in food analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Amantadine (AMD) is an amine derivative of saturated adamantine and it was once extensively used in the poultry industry for its antiviral ability by interfering with the M2 viral protein. But the abuse of AMD would cause severe consequences for human health. If AMD residues entered and accumulated in human body via the food, it could cause dizziness, palpitation and other side effects [1]. Moreover, many AMD-resistant virus strains have also been reported [2]. Due to these concerns, it is forbidden to use AMD as an antiviral drug in the poultry industry in China and USA since 2005 and 2006, respectively [3–5]. However, AMD is occasionally illegally used as prophylactic agents in poultry in China. Several instrumental methods have been developed for the detection of AMD including gas chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography and liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry [6–8]. However, these methods usually required expensive equipment, complicated and long-time pretreatment for food samples and hence they were inappropriate for the rapid screening of AMD. Therefore, the development of a rapid, sensitive, accurate and economical method for the detection of AMD residues was necessary.

Immunoassays based on the highly-speciﬁc reaction between antigen and antibody are powerful and alternative tools for detecting chemical contaminants in foodstuff due to their high-sensitivity, low-cost, portability and high throughput screening ability. [9] Currently, several immunoassays for the rapid detection of AMD including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [10], immunochromatographic assay (ICA) [11], colorimetric ELISA [12] and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) immunosensor [13] have been reported. However, the assay sensitivities of most reported rapid approaches could not meet the requirements of detecting AMD in foodstuffs at trace level.

As a rapid, high-throughput, and cost-effective immunoassay, ELISA has been wildly used for the detection of chemical contaminants in food. Conventional ELISA characteristically involves enzymes and corresponding substrates which produce absorbance signals to indicate the presence of analytes. Replacing the absorbance signal with fluorescence signal is a simple and promising way to improve the sensitivity of competitive ELISA due to the considerable higher signal to noise (S/N) ratios of fluorescence signals. Recently, various improved ELISA strategies employed quantum dots (QDs) and other fluorescent nanomaterials as signal probes were developed to detect chemical contaminant in foodstuffs with higher sensitivity [14–16]. For instance, Zhang et al. achieved the rapid and sensitive detection of morphine in soup through QDs-based fluorescence-linked immunosorbent assay (FLISA) [14]. Zhang et al. developed a magnetic nanobeads-based FLISA for zearalenone detection in cereals using QDs as the probe [15]. Luo et al. used fluorescent silicon nanoparticles to develop a fluorescent ELISA for sensitive detection of ethyl carbamate in red wine [16]. Xiong’s group developed highly sensitive fluorescent ELISAs for the detection of ochratoxin A and zearalenone by catalase-mediated fluorescence quenching of QDs [17,18]. Nevertheless, traditional fluorescent nanomaterials like QDs are expensive and toxic which limit their further application.

Compared with the traditional semiconductor QDs, carbon dots (CDs) have many attractive advantages including excellent photoluminescence (PL) stability, simple synthesis, low cost, non-toxicity and good biocompatibility. As a kind of promising green substitute for the semiconductor QDs, CDs have been increasingly used as the signal probes in immunoassays [19–22]. However, CDs were mainly employed as fluorescent labels to substitute enzymes (called FLISA). The FLISA sacrifices the signal amplification of enzymatic reactions, usually resulting in comparable sensitivity as that of ELISA. To achieve higher sensitivity, a new fluorescence immunoassay should have capability to take the advantages of both the enzymatic amplification and fluorescent signal readout.

Herein, we have established a new fluorescence immunoassay which uses the nanoassembly of CDs and MnO2 NSs as the fluorescence probe (Scheme1 ). In this strategy, we prepare the nanoassembly of MnO2 NSs and CDs through the strong electrostatic interaction. The Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and MnO2 NSs would lead to the ﬂuorescence suppression of CDs after the formation of the nanoassembly. Meanwhile, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) could catalyze the hydrolysis of 2-phosphoascorbic acid (AAP) to generate ascorbic acid (AA). The produced AA could reduce MnO2 into Mn2+ and cause the decomposition of MnO2 NSs [23], followed by releasing the CDs from the surface of the MnO2 NSs. Based on the above signal generation mechanism, a ﬂuorescence ELISA was successfully developed for highly sensitive monitoring of AMD in chicken tissues.

ALP, 2-Phosphoascorbic acid trisodium salt (AAP),Ascorbic acid (AA), polyethyleneimine branched (PEI, MW = 10,000) and tris-(hydroxymethyl)- aminomethane (Tris) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). KMnO4, sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and citric acid monohydrate were achieved from Sinopharm Chem. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Alkaline Phosphatase AffiniPure Goat Anti-Mouse IgG were purchased from Jackson ImmunoResearch Laboratories Inc. (West Grove, PA, USA). All other reagents of analytical reagent grade were purchased from Sinopharm Chem. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) without further purification. AMD, ribavirin, moroxydine, acyclovir, acetaminophen, enrofloxacin, and sulfamethazine were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Chloramphenicol was obtained J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). All reagents were used without further puriﬁcation. Anti-AMD monoclonal antibody (mAb, 7G2) and coating antigen (BSA-AMD) were previously produced by our research group [10]. 96-well microplates were bought from Costar Inc. (Cambridge, MA, USA). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) was prepared by a Millipore water puriﬁcation system (Merck KGaA, Germany).

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using FS-5 spectroﬂuorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) spectrophotometer equipped with a UV detector. Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were measured on a FS-5 spectroﬂuorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) spectrophotometer equipped with a fluorescence detector. The absolute emission quantum yield of CDs was tested using an integrating sphere attached to an FS-5 spectroﬂuorometer. Fluorescence lifetime was measured with a Horiba DeltaFlex (Jobin Yvon, France). Fluorescence intensity measure was performed in 96-well plates on a PerkinElmer Envision plate reader (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were carried out on a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (FEI NanoPorts, USA) operated at 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) characterization was performed on a Dimension Icon (Bruker, USA) using a tapping mode. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet IS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) with the KBr pellet technique. Zeta potential measurements were performed with a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, USA). Raman spectra were recorded using inVia Raman microscope (Renishaw, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded using Escalab 250Xi (Thermo Scientific, USA). Fluorescent photos were taken using a SgaeCapture fluorescence imaging system (Sage Science, Beijing) under the excitation of a UV lamp (365 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the nanoassembly of CDs and MnO2 NSs
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of the PEI-functionalized CDs (p-CDs): [24,25] Citric acid (0.30 g), branched PEI (0.80 g) and 20 mL water were mixed, and then transferred into a 50 mL Teﬂon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove the precipitation. Then, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and then dialyzed against distilled water in the dialysis membrane (8000 Da) for 48 h to obtain p-CDs solution. The solution was freeze-dried in vacuum freeze dryer to obtain the solid powder of p-CDs.

Preparation of the MnO2 NSs [26,27]: Single-layer MnO2 NSs were one-step synthesized with the assistant of SDS in an aqueous solution. Briefly, a diluted H2SO4 solution (1.8 M, 45 μL) and SDS (461.4 mg) were sequentially added into 50 mL distilled water and heated at 95 °C for 15 min at first. KMnO4 solution (0.05 M, 1.6 mL) was rapidly added to the above SDS solution, and the temperature of the mixture solution was reduced to 85 °C. The mixture was under stirring at 85 °C for about 1 h, after that a dark brown solution was obtained. The synthesized MnO2 NSs were puriﬁed by centrifugation and wash with ultrapure water for three times. The sediment was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 6 h.

The p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly was prepared as follow: p-CDs aqueous solution (pH = 6, 20 μg mL−1) was introduced into MnO2 NSs aqueous solution (1 mg mL−1) under stirring. The mixture was adequately shaken for 2 h. The resultant mixture was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 20 min) and washed with ultrapure water two times. The as-obtained precipitate was re-dispersed in ultrapure water (50 μg mL−1) for the further usage.

100 μL/well BSA-AMD in a coating buffer (CB) were added into the 96-well microtitre plates and incubated overnight at 4 °C. The plates were washed two times with PBST and blocked with 150 μL of 2% casein in PBS and then incubated at 37 °C for 1.5 h. After three times of the subsequent washing steps, 50 μL per well of a series of AMD standard solution and 50 μL per well of anti-AMD mAb were added to each well, respectively. Followed by 0.5 h of incubation at 37 °C, the plates were washed with PBST three times, and 100 μL of ALP-labeled anti-mouse IgG (diluted 1:2500) was added to each well and then incubated for another 0.5 h at 37 °C. After washing for three times, 100 μL of the AAP solution (10 mM) was added to each well. The enzymatic reaction lasted for 40 min at 37 °C. Then 50 μL well−1 of the diluted nanoassembly solution (100 μg mL−1) were added into the microtitre plates. The PL intensity was measured after 5 min at room temperature. The standard curves were fitted: Y = (A − B)/[1+(X/C) D] +B. In the equation, A and B are the responses at high and low asymptotes of the curve, respectively. C is the concentration of the target resulting in half inhibition. D is the slope at the inflection point of the sigmoid and X is the target concentration.

Sample pretreatment is similar to our previous report [13]. Brieﬂy, chicken meat (2.0 g) was placed in a 10 mL polypropylene centrifuge tube and mixed with 2.0 mL of 70% methanol (v/v, contain 1% acetic acid). Then the mixture was homogenized for 5 min and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane and diluted 10 times with Tris buffer before analysis.

Scheme 1a briefly illustrates the preparation of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly as the probe used in the ﬂuorescent ELISA (the detailed description is in Section 3.2). The procedures of the proposed ﬂuorescent immunoassay are shown in Scheme 1b. Initially, the coating antigen (BSA-AMD) was coated on a 96-well microplate. A competitive immunoreaction occurred among the anti-AMD mAbs, coating antigen and AMD molecules in the solution. The anti-AMD mAbs could bind to the coating antigen adsorbed on the microplate through the specific antigen-antibody recognition. The immunocomplex was formed after the ALP-labeled secondary antibody was introduced. The presence of AMD in the solution could competitively bind with anti-AMD mAbs, and resulting in less amount of ALP-labeled immunocomplex. The ALP could catalyze the hydrolysis of AAP into AA which would reduce the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly and release the p-CDs. The ﬂuorescence intensity of the system increased with the destruction of the MnO2 NSs. In this competitive immunoassay, the change of the fluorescence intensity depended on the concentration of AMD in the solution quantitatively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly
ce:section.ce:para: Water-soluble MnO2 NSs of single-layer were synthesized by reducing KMnO4. SDS plays as the precursor for reductant and structure director in the preparation of the MnO2 NSs. The MnO2 NSs had an intense UV–vis absorption from 300 nm to 600 nm and exhibited a maximum absorption peak at 385 nm which is the characteristic of MnO2 nanomaterials (Fig. 1 a). The TEM image of MnO2 NSs showed a typical two-dimensional morphology (Fig. 1b). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of MnO2 NSs was shown in Fig. S1, which revealed crystal lattice fringes of 0.31 nm, consistent with the (110) plane of the MnO2 crystal [28]. The morphology of the MnO2 NSs was also characterized by AFM. As shown in Fig. 1c, MnO2 NSs displayed an average thickness of 1.1 nm, indicating their single-layer sheet structure. High-resolution XPS spectra of MnO2 NSs (Fig. S2) showed two characteristic peaks located at 642.0 and 83.4 eV, corresponding to Mn2p and Mn3s peaks, respectively. These experimental results were consistent with previous reported results and indicated the successful preparation of the MnO2 NSs.

p-CDs were obtained by a hydrothermal reaction. Here, PEI is an abundant amine groups-containing polymer with repeating ethylenediamine units, and it also acted as the surface passivating and functionalizing agent during the formation of p-CDs. The surface passivation of CDs by PEI could significantly enhance the fluorescence properties of p-CDs and make p-CDs positively charged [24,29]. We investigated the PL spectrum of the p-CDs (Fig. 1d). Under the excitation of 360 nm, the p-CDs solution exhibited strongest blue emission with a fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of 41.7%. The TEM image (Fig. 1e) showed that p-CDs were monodisperse spherical nanoparticles with diameters of 4 nm to 8 nm. The p-CDs had a graphitic structure with a lattice spacing of 0.30 nm (upper inset in Fig. 1e), which matches with the (002) lattice spacing of graphite. AFM image of p-CDs in Fig. 1f showed a uniform height distribution of the CDs, showing the average heights of 3.1 nm. High-resolution XPS spectrum of the p-CDs was shown in Fig. S3. All the features of the p-CDs we made were similar to the previous reports.

The p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly formed through the strong electrostatic interaction between positively charged p-CDs and negatively charged MnO2 NSs. In order to optimize the load rate of p-CDs in the nanoassembly, diﬀ ;erent concentrations of p-CDs were introduced into MnO2 NSs solution. As shown in Fig. S4a, with the increasing p-CDs concentration from 0.5 to 30 μg mL−1, the quenching eﬃciency decreased, correspondingly. The high concentration of p-CDs (30 μg mL−1) could cause the precipitation of the MnO2 NSs due to the strong electrostatic interaction. Thus, 20 μg mL−1 of the p-CDs and 1.0 mg mL−1 of MnO2 NSs were selected as the optimized ratio for the preparation of the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly. The formation of p-CDs@MnO2 was validated by dynamic light scattering (DLS). We found that the average zeta potential of the MnO2 NSs increased from −36.6 mV to −21.3 mV after the formation of the nanoassembly (Fig. 2 a). The increase of the zeta potential was due to the electrostatically attaching of p-CDs onto negatively charged MnO2 NSs. Moreover, the obtained nanoassembly was still negatively charged and showed good dispersibility and fluorescence stability in water after 20 days. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of p-CDs@MnO2 NSs ranged from 82.5% to 87.8 % within 20 days (Fig. S4 b). The average hydrodynamic size of p-CDs@MnO2 (41.1 μm) obviously enlarged compared to the bare MnO2 NSs (12.2 μm), too (Fig. 2b). The slight aggregation of MnO2 NSs caused by p-CDs was the reason for this enlargement. The DLS results confirmed that p-CDs were successfully adsorbed on the surface of MnO2 NSs through the electrostatic interaction.

As displayed in Fig. 3 a, the ﬂuorescence intensity of p-CDs at 450 nm decreased gradually after the formation of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly because p-CDs were easily adsorbed on the MnO2 surface to form the nanoassembly. We used TEM, AFM, Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum, Raman and XPS spectra to confirm the formation of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly. TEM images of the p-CDs@MnO2 showed that the nanoassembly had a multilayered structure (Fig. 3b and Fig. S5). The HRTEM image of the nanoassembly (Fig. 3c) indicated the adhesion of p-CDs on the MnO2 surface. AFM image of the nanoassembly in Fig. 3d showed a height distribution about 12 nm which was well consistent with the multilayered structure found in TEM image. The FT-IR characteristic peaks of p-CDs (1705 cm−1, and 1580 cm−1 for the C = O and N–H bonds) and MnO2 (507 cm−1 for MnO bonds) simultaneously appeared in the spectra belonged to p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly (Fig. 3e). Furthermore, this spectral phenomenon was also veriﬁed by Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectra of the MnO2 NSs exhibited characteristic peaks at 574 cm−1 and 653 cm−1, which were attributed to the MnO vibration (Fig. 3f, black line). The p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly showed three characteristic peaks located at 574 cm−1 (MnO2 NSs), 653 cm−1 (MnO2 NSs) and 755 cm−1 (p-CDs) (Fig. 3f, red line), which further confirmed the formation of the nanoassembly. The XPS spectra further proved the successful deposition of the p-CDs on the surface of MnO2 NSs. The XPS spectra of p-CDs exhibited only signals from the elements of C, N, and O (black line in Fig. 3g). However, the Mn2p and Mn3s peaks occurred in the XPS spectra of nanoassembly (red line in Fig. 3g, Fig. S2). UV–vis spectra of the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly was displayed in Fig. S6a. Compared with the bare MnO2 NSs, the UV–vis spectra of the nanoassembly had a blue-shift which was caused by the adsorption of p-CDs. All these characterizations confirmed the successful synthesis of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of ALP based on the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly
ce:section.ce:para: MnO2 NSs is a typical kind of 2D nanomaterials which has large surface area and superior light absorption capability (the molar extinction coefﬁcient is 9.6 × 103 M−1 cm−1 at 380 nm), et al [30,31]. These properties make MnO2 NSs the promising ﬂuorescence quenchers [32–34]. The MnO2–CDs have been applied in developing ﬂuorescence sensing systems based on the fluorescence “switch-off-on” strategy. [35–39]. As depicted in Fig. 2a, there was a broad overlap between the absorbance spectra of MnO2 NSs and the ﬂuorescence spectra of p-CDs. This spectra overlap is an indispensable factor for the FRET between p-CDs and MnO2 NSs, causing the efficient fluorescence quenching of p-CDs. The results of TEM and DLS also the successful deposition of the p-CDs on the surface of MnO2 NSs, which could shorten and restrict the distance between p-CDs and MnO2 NSs to generate efficient FRET. Taking this into consideration, we hypothesize that the efficient FRET occurs between MnO2 NSs and p-CDs. To further confirm this hypothesis, we measured the lifetimes of p-CDs and p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly. The lifetime of p-CDs decreased from 5.61 ns to 3.57 ns after the formation of p-CDs@MnO2 nanocomposite (Fig. 4 a). Thus, FRET contributed to the fluorescence quenching of p-CDs after the formation of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly. Moreover, the FRET was achieved through a facile electrostatic self-assembly of the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly. Additional functionalization of p-CDs and MnO2 NSs was avoided.

It has been reported that AA could reduce MnO2 NSs into Mn2+ due to their strong oxidative capacity [23]. Thus, the quenched fluorescence of p-CDs could be recovered when AA was introduced into the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly solution causing the reducing of MnO2 NSs. As presented in Fig. S6a, the UV–vis spectra of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly changed obviously after the addition of AA. The disappearance of the UV–vis characteristic peak indicated the decomposition of MnO2 NSs. The fluorescence of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly was restored after being reduced by AA. Fig. 4b and Fig. S6b showed a gradual increase of the fluorescence intensity of p-CDs accompanying with increasing concentration of AA in the solution. Besides, both AA and Mn2+ in the solution had almost no eﬀ ;ect on the ﬂuorescence intensity of p-CDs (Fig. S7). We also investigated the influence of pH conditions on the reduction of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly by AA (Fig. S8a). Considering the optimal alkaline pH for ALP activity, pH 9.0 was chose as the reaction condition although the AA had stronger reducibility for the nanoassembly under neutral pH

As an enzyme having high catalytic activity and broad substrate specificity, ALP could catalyze the hydrolysis of AAP to AA. The AA could reduce the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly and restore the fluorescence of p-CDs. It is proposed that p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly could be used for the sensitive detection of ALP. As presented in Fig. 4c, the ﬂuorescence intensity of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly was continuously restored along with the increasing of ALP concentration in solution. Meanwhile, the ﬂuorescence intensity of the system was related with ALP concentration. There was a linear relationship between ﬂuorescence intensity and ALP concentrations ranging from 0 to 80 mU mL−1 (F = 53.88 + 62.89 C [ALP] (mU mL−1), R2 = 0.986). The detection limit of 0.10 mU mL−1 for ALP was obtained according to the calculation of three times the standard deviation. Compared with the colorimetric method (LOD of ALP activity was 0.9 mU mL–1, Fig. S8b), the ﬂuorescent assay of ALP activity had higher sensitivity, which is fundamental for constructing a highly sensitive fluorescent ELISA. Based on the successful ALP sensing, the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly could be employed in further ALP-based ELISA for rapid screening and quantification of target analytes.

In our research, we employed the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly in developing an indirect competitive fluorescence ELISA. For an indirect competitive ELISA, the concentrations of coating antigen and antibody were two crucial factors that determine the sensitivity of ELISA. The concentrations of coating antigen and antibody for the fluorescence ELISA were optimized through checkerboard titration to achieve the optimum parameters according to the competitive inhibition rates. The inhibition rates were obtained using the following equation: (F0 − F/F0) × 100%, where F0 and F stand for the fluorescence intensity of the negative sample and an AMD-spiked buffer (0.3 ng mL−1) in the fluorescent ELISA, respectively. The optimum working concentrations of coating antigen (BSA- AMD) and mAb 7G2 were 0.04 μg mL−1 and 0.025 μg mL−1) for fluorescent ELISA, respectively (Table S1). Under the optimized conditions, the standard curves for the detection of AMD were established and showed in Fig. 5 a. Using p-CDs@MnO2 as the probe, highly sensitive ELISA with fluorescence signal output is successfully developed. The half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) and the LOD (defined as IC10) of the novel fluorescent ELISA were 0.23 ng mL−1 and 0.035 ng mL−1, respectively. The analytical performance of fluorescent ELISA was significantly improved than that of ALP-based conventional ELISA (IC50 was 1.25 ng mL−1 and LOD was 0.2 ng mL−1). To further express the advantages of our fluorescence immunoassay, the analytical properties of our method were compared with other reported immunoassays for AMD detection and summarized in Table 1 . The as-developed fluorescence ELISA had a lower LOD than most of the reported strategies. The improvement of the assay sensitivity was attributed to the successful signal ampliﬁcation using the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly as the probe: The high molar extinction coefficient and the broad absorption spectra of the MnO2 NSs could efficiently quench the fluorescence of p-CDs through FRET. The MnO2 NSs have strong oxidative capacity, and the hydrolyze AA by ALP could reduce the MnO2 NSs and restore the fluorescence of p-CDs. Thus, the fluorescence ELISA with a high S/N ratio was successfully developed and showed significantly improvement in sensitivity.

The fluorescence immunoassay could meet the requirement of AMD monitoring in foodstuff. Standard curves were developed in diluted matrix solutions containing different amount of methanol. The effect of the matrix and methanol content on the sensitivity of fluorescence ELISA was evaluated and showed in Fig. S9. The result of the IC50 values indicated that the 10-fold diluted extract (containing 7% methanol) had eliminated the matrix effect for fluorescent ELISA. The precision and reproducibility of the fluorescence ELISA were studied by evaluating the intra-batch and inter-batch assays. The speciﬁcity of the ﬂuorescence ELISA was evaluated against several non-target analytes including ribavirin, moroxydine, acyclovir, acetaminophen, chloramphenicol, enrofloxacin, and sulfamethazine (all 1.0 ng mL−1). As shown in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensities of the non-target analyte spiked samples were approximately same to that of the blank sample. These results showed good selectivity and speciﬁcity of the developed fluorescent ELISA for AMD detection. Chicken sample spiked with AMD at the concentration of 0.6, 3.0 and 10.0 ng mL−1 were detected by fluorescent ELISA. As shown in Fig. 5c, the average recoveries for intra-assay (n = 3) and inter-assay (n = 6) ranged from 72.6% to 90.4% with an acceptable coefﬁcient of variation (CV) under 13.2%. The results indicate that the developed fluorescence ELISA showed a great potential to be applied in detecting trace chemical contaminants in food.

In this study, a new fluorescence immunoassay for the sensitive detection of AMD was established with improved analytical performance. The p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly was prepared and used as the fluorescence substrate. AA as the ALP-mediated hydrolysis product of AAP could effectively restore the fluorescence of p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly. Then the new ALP-based fluorescence immunoassay was developed on the basis of the ELISA system using nanoassembly as the fluorescence probe. The new ﬂuorescence ELISA had higher sensitivity for AMD detection, exhibited the dynamic linear detection of AMD from 0.048 ng mL−1 to 1.1 ng mL−1 and LOD of 0.035 ng mL−1. The developed method also had good accuracy, precision and high specificity. In summary, the developed ﬂuorescent immunoassay can be used for the rapid and sensitive detection of trace AMD and this approach would have a great potential to be employed in the detection of other analytes in food analysis.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financed by the National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFE0110800), National Natural Science Foundation of China (31622057) and Sanming Project of Medicine in Shenzhen (SZSM201611068).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Additional information related to this work including the HRTEM image of the MnO2 NSs, Fig. S1. The XPS spectra of the MnO2 NSs, Fig. S2. The XPS spectra of the p-CDs, Fig. S3. The optimization for the formation of the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly and fluorescence stability of the nanoassembly, Fig. S4. The TEM images of the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly, Fig. S5. The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra changes of the p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly, Fig. S6. The normalized ﬂuorescence intensity stability of p-CDs and p-CDs@MnO2 nanoassembly, Fig. S7 and S8. IC50 values in the different diluted sample matrix, Fig. S9. The checkerboard data for fluorescent ELISA, Table S1.
